The Italian PO in Ottoman Tripoli closed when Italy annexed Libya in 1912. Postally the interval 1912-1923 was covered by 'Libia', which is absent from this file, so the link to Tripolitania skips an intermediate entity. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Kolchak's 4 Jan 1920 decree transferred the title of Supreme Ruler of Russia to Denikin, making Denikin the nominal successor White authority. Fernando Poo's own issues ceased in 1909 (apart from a 1929 issue) when Spanish Guinea / Spanish Territories of the Gulf of Guinea stamps took over. Fernando Poo issues resumed 1960, so the file's 1868-1968 span conceals this interruption. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
In 1960 Spanish Guinea was reorganised into the two Spanish provinces of Fernando Poo and Río Muni, each issuing separately. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Constitutional change of 1861 renamed the Granadine Confederation the United States of New Granada; the September 1861 issue is inscribed 'Estados Unidos de Nueva Granada'. Same polity, same postal authority, new name. Successor state (United States of Colombia, from 1862) is not present in this file, so the chain terminates at New Granada. Between 1950-51 Colombian domestic airmail was split between two carriers, each identified by overprint ('L' for LANSA, 'A' for AVIANCA). LANSA became an Avianca subsidiary by end of 1951 and the dual-overprint system ended; unoverprinted Colombian airmail stamps resumed. Full corporate integration was not completed until 1954, so no precise postal transition date is asserted. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Italian forces entered Addis Ababa 5 May 1936; annexation proclaimed 9 May 1936 and Ethiopia absorbed into Italian East Africa. The 1936 'Etiopia' overprints on Italian stamps were a military/colonial postal administration, not a legal successor — the annexation was never recognised by most of the international community and Haile Selassie's government continued in exile. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

British and Ethiopian forces liberated Addis Ababa; Haile Selassie restored 5 May 1941. Ethiopian postal administration resumed (initially alongside British military administration issues, which are not entities in this file). [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Leopold II's personal state annexed by Belgium 15 Nov 1908; first Belgian Congo stamps 1909. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Belgian Congo became the independent Republic of the Congo (Leopoldville/Kinshasa) on 30 June 1960 — the entity listed as "Zaire" [1960-1971] in this file. Note the file contains two entities both named exactly "Zaire"; this edge targets the 1960-1971 one. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Katanga seceded 11 July 1960, days after Congolese independence, and ran its own postal administration. Secession was never internationally recognised; Congo (Leopoldville) regarded the territory as its own throughout. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
South Kasai (Bakwanga) declared autonomy Aug 1960; its own stamps appeared 1961. Unrecognised; claimed by the central Congolese state. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Katangan secession collapsed Jan 1963; territory and postal service reintegrated into the Congo (Leopoldville) — the "Zaire" [1960-1971] entity. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
South Kasai crushed and reabsorbed by the central Congolese government in late 1962; successor is the "Zaire" [1960-1971] entity. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Congo (Kinshasa) renamed Republic of Zaire on 27 Oct 1971 — same polity and postal administration, new name. NOTE: the group file lists both periods under the identical string "Zaire" ([1960-1971] and [1971-present]), so predecessor and successor names are textually identical; they are distinct entities in the file. Belgian trust territory partitioned at independence 1 July 1962; Rwanda is one of two successors. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Belgian trust territory partitioned at independence 1 July 1962; Burundi is the other successor. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Self-proclaimed Republic of Serbian Krajina overrun by Operation Storm, Aug 1995; Croatian post resumed. Never internationally recognised; Serb authorities disputed Croatian sovereignty throughout. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

After Storm, only the eastern sector (Eastern Slavonia/Baranja/Western Syrmia) remained under Serb administration and continued issuing separately under UNTAES. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
UNTAES-administered Eastern Slavonia peacefully reintegrated into Croatia 15 Jan 1998; local issues ceased 1997 and Croatian Post took over. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Turkish intervention July 1974 split the island; separate Turkish Cypriot postal issues from 1974/75, TRNC declared 1983. Recognised only by Turkey; the Republic of Cyprus claims the whole island and treats northern issues as invalid. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Guanacaste was a Costa Rican province using overprinted issues 1885-89; the separate provincial issuing arrangement ended and ordinary Costa Rica stamps resumed. Administrative, not a change of sovereignty. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Short-lived separate Galapagos (Archipielago de Colon) issues 1957-59 ceased; the islands reverted to ordinary Ecuadorian stamps. Always Ecuadorian territory — no sovereignty change. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Colony separated 1 Oct 1975; separate Gilbert Islands stamps from 1976. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Ellice Islands separated as Tuvalu 1 Oct 1975; own postal administration from Jan 1976. Independent 1978. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Independence as Kiribati; effectively also a rename of the same postal authority. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Autonomous principality; union with Greece 1913, Samos issues ceased 1915 when Greek stamps took over. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Short-lived Free State of Ikaria 1912; formally annexed to Greece, Greek stamps thereafter. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Greek Balkan-War occupation overprints; island ceded to Greece by Treaty of London/Lausanne and Greek stamps followed. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Kavala awarded to Greece by Treaty of Bucharest 1913; occupation overprints replaced by ordinary Greek stamps. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Greek military administration 1947 issues; formal union with Greece 1947–48, Greek stamps thereafter. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Autonomous Republic of Northern Epirus issues ended with Greek occupation 1916, but the territory was ultimately assigned to Albania (not an entity in this file). Succession genuinely disputed. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Transfer of sovereignty to the PRC; the postal administration continued unbroken as Hongkong Post under the new name. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Indonesian administration from 1963; Act of Free Choice 1969, separate West Irian issues ceased 1970 in favour of Indonesian stamps. Legitimacy of the 1969 act is disputed. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Separate Riau-currency overprints for the archipelago ended when the special currency zone was abolished and ordinary Indonesian stamps came into use. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The Isle of Man Post Office Authority took over the postal assets and functions of the British Post Office on 5 July 1973; from that day only Manx stamps were valid on the island. Prior to that the island was served by GB regional issues (from 18 Aug 1958). GB (Regional Issues) continues for Scotland/Wales/N. Ireland, so this is a partial, territorial succession. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The States of Guernsey Post Office Board took over postal administration of the Bailiwick from the GB Post Office on 1 Oct 1969, ending the GB regional issues used there from 1958. Note the 'Guernsey' entity as listed begins in 1941 with the German occupation issues, so it overlaps its own predecessor; this edge models the 1969 transfer of postal authority only. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Jersey's postal administration passed from the GB Post Office to the States of Jersey on 1 Oct 1969, ending the GB regional issues used there from 1958. As with Guernsey, the listed entity period (from 1941) overlaps the regional period; the edge models the 1969 transfer only. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The League dissolved itself on 18 April 1946, transferring its assets and the Palais des Nations to the UN. Philatelically the Swiss PTT's 'Societe des Nations' official issues gave way to United Nations (Geneva) officials. Not a clean 1:1 legal succession - several League organs passed to different bodies - but the postal establishment at Geneva passed directly to the UN. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

predecessor: Postage of the International Education Office
notes: The International Bureau of Education (Geneva), founded 1926, became an institute of UNESCO in 1969, and its Swiss official stamp series ended with the merger. The listed date ranges (IEO ending 1960, UNESCO beginning 1961) reflect catalogue listing periods rather than the institutional merger date; I used the merger date and flag the discrepancy. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The 1908 King Edward VII Land overprints were a one-off expedition post office (Shackleton's Nimrod) using New Zealand stamps. The territory was incorporated into the Ross Dependency by the Order in Council of 30 July 1923; NZ-administered Ross Dependency stamps followed in 1957. Low confidence: territorial/administrative continuity under the same NZ postal authority rather than a true succession - the expedition office simply closed, with a 45-year gap. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

As with King Edward VII Land: the 1911-12 Victoria Land overprints were an expedition post office (Scott's Terra Nova) using New Zealand stamps, in territory later constituted as the Ross Dependency (1923). Same low-confidence caveat - geographic and authority continuity, not a postal succession per se. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Same protectorate administration renamed Niger Coast Protectorate in May 1893; stamps continue directly. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Niger Coast Protectorate became the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria on 1 Jan 1900 (absorbing Royal Niger Company southern territories). NCP stamps remained in use/issue until 1902, overlapping the first Southern Nigeria issues of 1901. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Colony of Lagos amalgamated with the Protectorate of Southern Nigeria to form the Colony and Protectorate of Southern Nigeria; Lagos postal administration absorbed. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Not a direct hand-over: the 1944-48 'Muscat' issues were Indian-administration overprints; the British Postal Agency ran the service 1 Apr 1948 - 29 Apr 1966 (that intervening entity is not in this file). The Sultanate assumed postal control on 30 Apr 1966. Same territory, but an 18-year gap under a third administration. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Sultanate renamed Oman after the 1970 accession of Qaboos; first stamps inscribed Sultanate of Oman issued 16 Jan 1971. Same postal authority. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
British annexation during the Second Boer War; OFS stamps overprinted V.R.I. Boer government contested the annexation and fought on until 1902. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Princely state acceded to Pakistan 7 Oct 1947; own issues ended with the Oct 1949 UPU commemoratives, after which Pakistani stamps were required on all outgoing mail. Bahawalpur stamps stayed valid internally to 1953; state merged into West Pakistan 1955. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

predecessor: US post in the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands
notes: Palau Postal Service took over from the US postal operation in the Trust Territory and began its own issues 10 Mar 1983; full independence came 1994. The Marshall Islands and Micronesia are co-successors of the same TTPI postal operation but are not in this file. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
Papua Act 1905 transferred the territory to Australia as the Territory of Papua; BNG stamps overprinted PAPUA in 1906. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Both are the Australian military occupation postal administration of former German New Guinea; the New Britain G.R.I. overprints were superseded by the broader North West Pacific Islands issues. German New Guinea (the occupied predecessor) is not in this file. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Australian military occupation administration gave way to the civil administration of the League of Nations mandated Territory of New Guinea, which began its own issues in 1925. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Papuan issues ceased with the 1942 Japanese invasion; postally reunited with New Guinea under the joint Territory of Papua and New Guinea, first joint stamps 30 Oct 1952. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
New Guinea issues ceased in 1942; territory merged with Papua into the joint administration whose first stamps appeared 30 Oct 1952. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The Shaan-Gan-Ning border region post was the core of the communist administration that was reorganised into the Northwest China Liberation Area People's Post covering Shaanxi, Gansu, Ningxia, Qinghai and Xinjiang. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Congress Poland's own stamp (issued 1 Jan 1860) was withdrawn from use on 1 April 1865 following the January Uprising of 1863; Russian imperial stamps were mandatory from that day, absorbing the Kingdom into the Russian postal system. The file's period [1860-1863] understates actual usage, which ran to April 1865. No direct edge to 'Poland' [1918-] is asserted: 53 years of Russian imperial administration intervene. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Republic of Central Lithuania (Zeligowski's mutiny) parliament voted for union with Poland 20 Feb 1922; Poland accepted the annexation 24 Mar 1922 and Polish stamps replaced the Srodkowa Litwa issues. Contested: the elections were boycotted by most Lithuanian and Jewish voters and never recognised internationally; Lithuania regarded the Vilnius seizure as illegal and the two states remained in an undeclared state of war until 1938. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The March 1921 plebiscite led to the League of Nations partition of Upper Silesia; the eastern industrial zone passed to Poland with the Polish takeover beginning 20 June 1922, ending the plebiscite postal administration. Many-to-many: the larger western portion went to Germany, but no German entity appears in this file so that half of the partition is not emitted. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

Cieszyn/Teschen Silesia: the plebiscite was never actually held; the Conference of Ambassadors divided the territory 28 July 1920. Many-to-many: the larger share went to Czechoslovakia, which is not in this file, so only the Polish half is emitted. Poland disputed the award (Zaolzie) through 1938. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]

The exile government's stamps (used chiefly by the Polish merchant marine and forces abroad) ceased when the Western Allies withdrew recognition on 5 July 1945 in favour of the Provisional Government of National Unity in Warsaw. Contested: the exile government continued to exist in London until 1990 and disputed the Warsaw government's legitimacy, regarding itself as the continuator of the Second Republic. [UNVERIFIED: authored, skeptic did not run]
